author: @stevederp9801
text:
That’s because Hollywood doesn’t care about making movies, tv and music anymore. They care about pushing the message. So now the music industry has moved to Nashville, the film industry has moved to Georgia and TV can literally be made anywhere now. While the bureaucracy is definitely a huge issue making it hard to make movies and music industry LA. The bigger reality is that it’s far too expensive for young and talented actors and musicians to live. Van Halen, Metallica and Motley Crue all had a period where they shared a small crappy apartment and played shows on the weekends to get enough money to pay for their rent, food and partying. At the time getting an apartment in LA was something that could actually be realistic. Now if you had 4 talented musicians they would need to make $8,000 a month to afford rent and to live. There is absolutely no way that is going to be possible. So instead they move to Nashville where they can rent an apartment or house outside of the city and actually afford to live and there’s enough live music venues to actually make money and get exposure. The same goes for actors and actresses, why go to Hollywood where you have to work 2 jobs just to pay for a room to rent and have no time to audition and go to acting classes or improv. 

We’re seeing the exact same thing with comedy where the entire comedy scene is moving to Austin since they have an audience who will laugh at anything they say and not blacklist them for being on the wrong side. We’ve seen that LA and New York has had a lot of its talent move away to Austin seeing it as a better opportunity and a place they can actually afford to live. How can a young comedian try to live in New York when it’s $4,000 for rent. It’s only possible to chase your dreams in Austin where you can find something for $1,000 for rent. 

I think the worst result of this is that we’re seeing this huge wave of nepotism in Hollywood and New York. Where a huge amount of these new actors, comedians and musicians are related to other famous or influential people. Even Taylor swift had a father who was worth $200 million and moved to Nashville to launch her music career. Lady Gaga had a wealthy father and went to private school having the majority of her hit songs written for her by her producer redone. The results were are having is that a huge amount of the famous people we have are not the most talented but simply the most well connected.

author: @johanwester9079
text:
These videos is where you shine Gary! Yes there are all the aspects you mention and I know them from both my days in tinseltown as well as "Hollywood north" in Toronto/Canada. Lived and witnessed working within the industry globally for more than a decade!

However PRICE is the main factor driving the ticket sales down ever since 2002! It's irreversible when ANY business tries to get higher gross revenue from price increases (inflation) to battle declining sales amounts. 

Stupid People always do it no matter how educated in economics they are because they believe in eternal growth when eternal growth is just inflation at the end of the day! They believe they can beat the simplest rule of supply and demand but you kill demand with price increases and raise demand with price decreases! That's why there is the SALE and events like "black month" nowadays when Friday wasn't enough"

From 2002 as your graph points out ticket sales figures amounts not dollars have plummeted and just go look at prices not only in the US but worldwide in the same time frame. Regardless of plandemic we would end up here anyway but yes it also increased inflation when all businesses at the same time do the same stupid shit "to survive".

Here in Sweden, tickets have more than doubled in price since 2002, candy, soda and popcorn sold at the cinemas too. All the while cinemas just go emptier and emptier!

Factor in that most moviegoers also pay for streaming services, on average 4 subscriptions per household (and they too resort to the same stupid shit when growth stagnate they increase prices) consumers simply get more selective with what we are willing to pay for and what we get per "buck". We prioritize our budgets differently. I may still pay 100 bucks per year on cinema but it used to get me 10 shows now it gets me 4!

As long as you guys don't speak about the elephant in the room of pricing (yes some of it is due to ballooning budget cost but that still isn't the main driver) you actually will never give the full and honest picture about the problem or how to save it.

South East Asia still fills their seats at every showing I've been to there but their ticket prices are about 2 dollars not 20 or 30+. It is really that simple. All the other stuff doesn't help but it isn't what concerns the avg customer. The avg customer cares about their wallet

author: @AclypseOfReason
text:
Stupid anecdote but I live in Ottawa, Ontario. I worked for a private utility and the City of Ottawa raised their “cut permit” (a charge for cutting into asphalt roads) from under $200 to $490. 

I would email them and say the crew wasn’t cutting any asphalt as the job was in the grass in the side of the road and they still charged the same $490 regardless.

This utility paid over $100,000 every year for just road cut permits. It was a shake down. A hidden tax that would raise the price the utility would have to charge as they weren’t absorbing it.

Also this permit wasn’t to replace the asphalt as the utility still had to do that all themselves. It was for an inspector to make sure the crew did the reinstatement of the road or often the grass.
